title: Avoid oci-archive temporary extraction
body:
oci-archive format uses uncompressed tar file, so even if tar files are not indexed, we can do a first scan of the headers to build the index (skipping/seeking over the data) and build a fs.FS to then access it as if it was extracted.

We would need to refactor a bit the oci transport code to use fs.FS (and as a security improvement can use root.FS() https://pkg.go.dev/os@master#Root.FS), then we could use go-tarfs https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/nlepage/go-tarfs to open oci-archive

Any opinions ?
(Disclaimer: I haven't reviewed go-tarfs code)

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

`oci-archive:` actually transparently decompresses compressed files. It’s not a documented feature of `oci-archive`, but it is ~documented that `podman load` accepts compressed inputs, so we can’t entirely remove that functionality.

We do have a similar code that handles this for `docker/archive` (both transparently referring to a seekable input, and consuming tar files by reading headers), and that could probably be adapted here (but it would need to be made more general to correctly support symbolic and hard links).

The `go-tarfs` package, from a quick look, seems to be making unwarranted assumptions about (lack of) buffering in the `archive/tar` implementation, and it is completely missing handling of links and special files.

> Thanks for reaching out.
> 
> `oci-archive:` actually transparently decompresses compressed files. It’s not a documented feature of `oci-archive`, but it is ~documented that `podman load` accepts compressed inputs, so we can’t entirely remove that functionality.

good to know, we can keep the extract to temp dir code around

> We do have a similar code that handles this for `docker/archive` (both transparently referring to a seekable input, and consuming tar files by reading headers), and that could probably be adapted here (but it would need to be made more general to correctly support symbolic and hard links).
> 
> The `go-tarfs` package, from a quick look, seems to be making unwarranted assumptions about (lack of) buffering in the `archive/tar` implementation, and it is completely missing handling of links and special files.

I've opened a proposal on the go tracker, which was closed as duplicate of an older rejected proposal (https://github.com/golang/go/issues/74041), but it just led me to https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/quay/claircore/pkg/tarfs which seems saner than `go-tarfs`

Just had a look at https://github.com/containers/image/blob/main/docker/internal/tarfile/reader.go, we scan the tar each time we look for a file, `tarfs` would only scan once, and it handle symlink/hardlink already

At the relevant scale (less than a dozen files, usually), the cost of the scan should be trivially cheap, compared to all the I/O involved in reading contents. Adding an extra dependency (which carries an extra tar parser implementation) is not too attractive.

bootc defaults to 64 layers for chunked images, this can add up.
I've asked if we could have the offset of the last header: https://github.com/golang/go/issues/74041#issuecomment-2955689507

In any case if the current implementation is good enough for docker archive we could use it for oci-archive, and improve it later
